run_summary: Screen 1 holds 6 positions (all 12-13 days, nearing window max) with mixed recovery; two new exploratory-tier flags on infrastructure/healthcare names hit by loopy-agent sympathy. Prioritize exits on underwater positions approaching day 15.

entries.name: Phreesia, Inc.
entries.thesis: Agentic swarms target generic enterprise workflow automation; Phreesia's intake/engagement business is gated by HIPAA, EHR/PM integration plumbing, payer-specific eligibility, and life-sciences network contracts. A 'loopy' agent swarm cannot drop into a Cerner/Epic integration or take over patient-payment processing. The sympathy drop on a sub-average-volume day looks thematic, not fundamental.
entries.what_kills: An EHR vendor (Epic/Cerner) announcing native agentic intake; a Phreesia customer-cohort or net-retention deterioration in next print; a new risk-factor amendment naming agentic AI specifically.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: Classification OVERDONE, confidence 3 (meets min_buy_confidence). Threat_assessment 'minimal' (generic AI risk-factor boilerplate, no specific agentic-intake threat named in filings). Panic_calibration 'unjustified' with strong filings evidence: core moat (HIPAA, EHR/PM integration, payer eligibility, patient-contract lock-in) is non-substitutable by agentic swarms. Move is -4.9% on 0.7x volume, thematic sympathy. Basket rule does not apply (only 2 new flags). PHR gets exploratory tier (threat is 'minimal' not 'none', and confidence is exactly at threshold).

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case assumes HIPAA and EHR integration are durable moats, but does not address whether agentic AI vendors will simply partner with or acquire the integration layer rather than rebuild it.
red_team.critique: The thesis correctly identifies that HIPAA compliance and EHR plumbing are friction points, but conflates 'hard to do' with 'hard to bypass.' Agentic AI vendors facing this problem will not repeatedly fail to integrate—they will license, partner, or acquire the layer (as has happened throughout SaaS consolidation). The bull case also underweights the speed at which enterprise IT budgets shift: if CIOs see a 50% cost reduction via agentic intake, they will fund the integration work or swap vendors, regardless of legacy contracts. The -4.9% move on 0.7x volume is indeed thin and thematic, but that does not protect against a structural demand shift if the threat materializes.

entries.name: DigitalOcean Holdings, Inc.
entries.thesis: Agentic AI swarms that 'run continuously in the background' need somewhere to run — and DOCN's developer-cloud + GPU/inference offering is positioned as a host for exactly these workloads. Selling DOCN on an agent-swarm headline confuses the picks-and-shovels case with the workflow-software case. The drop on near-average volume is pure thematic SaaS-cloud sympathy.
entries.what_kills: A hyperscaler launching a developer-tier inference product priced below DOCN; a guide-down on AI/ML revenue; SMB customer-count deterioration.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: Classification OVERDONE, confidence 3 (meets min_buy_confidence). Threat_assessment 'minimal' (10-K explicitly positions AI/ML as growth vector; agentic swarms need compute hosting, not replacement). Panic_calibration 'unjustified' with filings evidence: DOCN is selling inference/GPU capacity *to* agentic workloads, not being displaced by them — picks-and-shovels case. Move is -4.06% on 0.97x volume, pure SaaS-cloud thematic sympathy. Basket rule does not apply (only 2 new flags). DOCN gets exploratory tier for same reason as PHR.

red_team.weakest_link: The picks-and-shovels framing assumes agentic AI workloads will remain distributed across developer clouds, but hyperscalers are moving inference in-house and offering it cheaper, which could compress DOCN's margin and growth.
red_team.critique: The bull case correctly notes that DOCN benefits from inference demand, but omits the price-competition vector: AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure are rapidly commoditizing inference and undercutting independent cloud providers on price and integration. If agentic workload operators choose hyperscaler-native inference over DOCN's developer cloud, top-line growth accelerates but unit economics worsen, and SMB developer churn accelerates. The -4.06% move on 0.97x volume is benign, but the structural margin pressure from hyperscaler competition is a real what-kills scenario that the bull case minimizes.
